# AI in the Terminal

Chapter 16 added a kit of faster, friendlier commands to the
shell. This chapter adds something different in kind: a program
that reads your plain-English request and then runs shell
commands for you. A terminal AI agent, Claude Code, OpenAI's
Codex CLI, or Google's Gemini CLI, sits at your prompt, proposes
edits and commands, and, depending on how you have set it up,
runs them. It is genuinely useful for the work this book is
about: scaffolding a project, writing a first draft of a
cleaning script, explaining an error, wiring a new step into a
Makefile. It is also the most powerful thing you have pointed at
your files, because it can do, on its own initiative, everything
the earlier chapters taught you to do carefully.

That is the whole reason this chapter exists, and why it comes
after the danger chapter and not before. An agent runs the same
shell you do. It can `rm -rf` the wrong directory, clobber a
file with `>`, or pipe a stranger's script into `sh`, the exact
mistakes Chapter 7 spent its length teaching you to fear, except
now a confident machine can make them faster than you can read.
So the durable skill is not which agent to install or which
buttons its current version shows. It is knowing where the
boundaries are: what the tool will do without asking, what it
must never do unsupervised, where your secrets can leak, and how
to keep an agent's help from quietly breaking the reproducible
pipeline the rest of this book builds. Those boundaries outlast
any release, and they are the subject here.

A note on where the material comes from, and a warning about its
shelf life. This is the fastest-aging chapter in the book, so it
is written principles-first and every tool-specific detail is
stamped "current as of 2026-07-01" and pinned to the tool's
official documentation (logged in `verification/chapter-17.md`).
The exact CLI versions behind that stamp are recorded on the
author's Mac in `transcripts/ch16-versions-mac.txt`: Claude Code
2.1.197, OpenAI Codex CLI 0.142.5, and Gemini CLI 0.49.0. The
permission flags this chapter names are checked against the tools'
official docs and their captured `--help` output
(`transcripts/ch16-help-mac.txt`). One nuance worth stating:
Codex's `--help` prints its full-bypass flag only in the long
`--dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox` form, but the shorter
`--yolo` alias its docs describe is real, a captured
`codex --yolo --version` is accepted and runs. Almost
nothing here is a live capture, by deliberate choice: an agent
session is non-deterministic and costs a real API call, so every
agent interaction shown below is labeled illustrative and is
**not** run, on the Mac or in this book's Linux sandbox, exactly
as Chapter 7 handled the one `curl | sh` it refused to execute.
The one real, executed demonstration is the `diff` in the review
section, captured AI-free in the sandbox
(`transcripts/ch16-review-diff.txt`). Treat every product detail
as a snapshot to re-verify, and the principles around it as the
part meant to last.

## What an agent in the terminal actually is {.tier-beginner}

Strip away the chat interface and a terminal agent is a loop:
it reads your request, decides on an action, and takes it, then
reads the result and repeats until the task is done. The actions
it can take are the ones you already know. It edits files. It
runs shell commands. It may make network requests. There is no
fourth magic power. Everything an agent does, it does through the
same file system and the same shell that Chapters 5 through 12
covered, with your user account's permissions and no others.

Keeping that in view is the whole mental model. The shell runs
commands (Chapter 2); Git versions files (the companion Git
book); an agent *decides which commands to run* and then hands
them to the shell, the same division of labor Make had in
Chapter 12, except an agent's decisions come from a language
model reading your prompt rather than from a dependency graph you
wrote. That difference is the point. A Makefile does exactly what
its rules say. An agent does what it infers you meant, which is
powerful when it infers right and dangerous when it infers wrong,
and either way it reaches the shell through the same door.

Because the door is the shell, the safety questions are not new
and mysterious; they are the old ones from Chapter 7, asked about
a new actor. What can it change without asking me? What can it
reach? Where does what I type end up? The rest of this chapter is
those questions, one at a time.

## Permission models: ask, allow, and auto {.tier-beginner}

Every terminal agent has a setting that controls how often it
stops to ask before acting. This is the single most important
thing to understand about any of these tools, and it is the
first thing to check after installing one. The names differ by
product and they change between releases, but the underlying
axis is stable, and it runs from most oversight to least:

| Posture | Claude Code | Codex CLI | Gemini CLI |
|---|---|---|---|
| Read / plan | `plan` | `read-only` | `plan` |
| Ask | `default` | `on-request` | `default` |
| Auto-edit | `acceptEdits` | auto preset | `auto_edit` |
| Trust all | `bypassPermissions` | `--yolo` | `yolo` |

Read the table as one idea in a few settings, not twelve things
to memorize. At one end the agent may only read and propose; at
the other it runs whatever it decides to run. In between are the
two postures you will actually live in: **ask**, where the agent
pauses for approval before each file edit or command, and
**auto-edit**, where it applies file edits on its own but still
stops before running shell commands. The safe default every one
of these tools ships with sits at the cautious end.

Codex is worth pausing on, because it spreads the same choice
across two settings you set independently: a *sandbox mode* (what
it may touch: `read-only`, `workspace-write`, or
`danger-full-access`) and an *approval policy* (when it must ask:
`untrusted`, `on-request`, or `never`, with the older `on-failure`
now deprecated). The Codex column above names the nearest single
setting for each posture. The two axes matter most at the bottom
row: `danger-full-access` opens the *sandbox* while a `never`
policy removes the *prompts*, and Codex bundles turning off both
into one switch, `--dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox`
(alias `--yolo`), which is the true "run anything, anywhere"
posture the table shows. Opening only one axis is not the same
thing: `danger-full-access` with approvals still on, or `-a never`
while the workspace sandbox stays in place, each stops short of
full trust. For the record, Codex starts a version-controlled
folder in its auto preset (workspace-write plus on-request) and a
non-version-controlled folder in read-only, current as of
2026-07-01.

The one thing worth burning into memory is that you set this
posture yourself, through a flag or a settings file, and the
agent cannot talk you out of it or raise its own permissions by
asking nicely in the chat. In Claude Code you pass
`--permission-mode` or set `defaultMode` in `settings.json`; in
Codex you pass `--sandbox` and `--ask-for-approval`; in Gemini
you pass `--approval-mode`. What an ask-mode prompt looks like is
the same everywhere in spirit:

```text
# illustrative Claude Code ask-mode prompt, not run here.
# Shape only; current as of 2026-07-01, re-verify against
# the tool you actually use.
Claude wants to run:  rm -rf data/clean/
  1. Yes
  2. Yes, and don't ask again this session
  3. No, and tell Claude what to do differently
```

Option 2 is the one to notice: it widens the agent's standing
permission for the rest of the session. That is convenient and it
is how oversight quietly erodes over a long task, which is why
the auto end of the table deserves its own caution later in this
chapter.

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
The divergence in this chapter is not macOS versus Linux; it is
tool versus tool, and version versus version. Each tool has a
one-flag "run without asking" switch, and they do not share a
name: `bypassPermissions` (or `--dangerously-skip-permissions`) in
Claude Code, `--yolo` (its alias for
`--dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox`) in Codex, and
`--yolo` / `yolo` in Gemini CLI. Codex is the one that *also*
exposes the two underlying axes separately, a sandbox mode and an
approval policy, so there `-a never` alone still leaves the
workspace sandbox in place. All are current as of 2026-07-01 and
all are likely to be renamed or resplit before long. This is why
the chapter teaches the posture, not the keyword: when you sit down
at any agent, the first task is to find which setting controls
approvals, and for Codex also which controls the sandbox, from
that tool's own `--help` and docs, not from memory or from this
table. A setting you assume rather than check is the one that
surprises you.
:::

## The destructive-command boundary {.tier-beginner}

An agent in ask mode is safe in the way a careful colleague is
safe: it shows you each command before running it. The danger
lives in what it can run *without* showing you, and that set is
larger than beginners expect the moment you move off the strict
default. Claude Code's `acceptEdits` mode, for instance,
auto-approves not just file edits but a list of common filesystem
commands, `mkdir`, `touch`, `mv`, `cp`, and `rm` among them,
inside the working directory, current as of 2026-07-01. That is a
reasonable convenience and it is also exactly the command from
Chapter 7 with no undo. An agent that mis-infers which directory
is stale can remove a real one, and in auto-edit mode it does not
stop to ask first.

The tools do build in guard rails, and they are worth knowing
precisely because they are real but partial. Claude Code never
auto-approves writes to a set of protected paths, your `.git`
directory, your shell startup files like `.zshrc`, its own
configuration, in any mode short of full bypass; and even in
bypass mode it refuses to start as root and still stops to
confirm a literal `rm -rf /` or `rm -rf ~`, current as of
2026-07-01. Codex keeps `.git` read-only inside its writable
workspace. These are helpful, and they are not a safety net you
can lean on, for the same reason Chapter 7 gave: preserve-root
guards the literal `/`, but nothing guards the wrong *project*
directory, the deletion that is syntactically ordinary and
semantically a disaster.

::: {.callout-caution .danger title="DANGER"}
An agent running with approvals turned off can run every
destructive command in Chapter 7, on its own reading of what you
meant. The catalog is unchanged: `rm -rf` on a path built from a
wrong assumption; `>` clobbering a file it meant to append to; a
`git reset --hard` or `git checkout .` that discards uncommitted
work an agent judged irrelevant. The new element is speed and
initiative, an agent can issue the command before you have parsed
its intent, and confidence, the explanation preceding a wrong
command is often perfectly plausible. Two rules keep this bounded.
First, do not run an agent with its approvals turned off (Claude
Code's `bypassPermissions`, Gemini's `yolo`, or Codex's `--yolo`,
which disables both its sandbox and approvals) anywhere that
holds data you cannot regenerate or have not committed; these
settings trade away exactly the pause that protects such data,
and the later section on what an agent must never run
unsupervised takes up how each tool frames the risk. Second, keep
the irreplaceable out of reach entirely, the raw data you are
licensed not to lose, covered in the scope section next.
Appendix A's recovery options apply to an agent's
`rm` exactly as to your own.
:::

## File and scope boundaries {.tier-advanced}

The second question, after "what can it do without asking," is
"where can it do it." Every one of these tools is built around a
working directory and treats the folder you launched it in as its
sandbox. Codex, current as of 2026-07-01, enforces this at the
operating-system level in its default `workspace-write` mode:
the agent can read broadly but write only inside the active
workspace, and it holds `.git`, `.agents`, and `.codex` read-only
even there. Gemini CLI can run tools inside a Docker, Podman, or
macOS Seatbelt sandbox, though, notably, its sandbox is *off* by
default and only switches on automatically when you engage its
yolo mode. The scoping is real, but its default strength varies
by tool, which is the first thing to check.

The scope you should care about maps onto the project layout the
companion Git book locks in, and it has two halves that are easy
to run together but are not the same. What an agent can *write* is
the tightly bounded one: in a workspace-scoped mode it changes
only files under the directory you launched it in. What it can
*read* is usually wider, a tool like Codex reads broadly while
writing only inside the workspace, and included-directory settings
widen it further. So launching the agent from the project
directory bounds what it can change to the project, but does not by
itself keep it from reading files elsewhere. The
reproducible-pipeline discipline still gives you a concrete
boundary to enforce: keep raw, licensed, or confidential data out
of the project tree entirely (referenced, not committed, as in
Chapter 12's synthetic-data argument), so that neither the write
scope nor the broader read scope can reach it, whether to delete
it in a cleanup or to pull it into a prompt.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
An agent's scope is the directory you start it in, so starting it
in the wrong directory quietly hands it the wrong scope. Launch
one from your home directory to "just ask a quick question" and,
in a writing or auto mode, its working directory is now your
entire home folder, dotfiles, SSH keys, every project at once.
The habit that prevents this is dull and reliable: `cd` into the
specific project first, confirm with `pwd`, and only then start
the agent, the same look-before-you-act rule Chapter 7 applied to
`rm`. If a tool offers a way to add directories to its scope
(Claude Code's `additionalDirectories`, Gemini's
`--include-directories`), treat each addition as widening the
blast radius and add the minimum.
:::

## Network access and the supply chain {.tier-advanced}

The third question is what the agent can reach beyond your disk.
An agent that can make network requests can download and run
code, which is the Chapter 7 supply-chain problem wearing a new
coat: the blind `curl | sh` this book refuses to run is a line an
agent can compose and execute on your behalf, fetching a script
over a network you do not control and running it with your
permissions before you have read it. The exposure runs the other
way too. An agent that reads a web page or a file and then acts on
what it read can be steered by text planted in that content, a
prompt injection, into fetching and following instructions that
were never yours.

This is why the cautious posture is to keep the agent off the
network unless a task needs it, and the tools lean that way.
Codex, current as of 2026-07-01, runs with network access turned
off unless you enable it explicitly in its workspace-write
configuration, and its own documentation warns that turning on
network access or web search exposes the agent to prompt
injection. Claude Code, in its default mode, treats a network
request as an action it stops to prompt you for rather than one it
takes silently. Whatever your tool's default, enable network
access only for a specific task that genuinely needs it, prefer a
tool's allowlist of trusted destinations over blanket access, and
remember that an agent fetching a dependency is subject to the
same verify-the-installer, check-the-checksum discipline Chapter 7
laid out for you, precisely because it is doing the fetch as you.

::: {.callout-caution .danger title="DANGER"}
Giving an agent network access plus a trust-everything approval
mode is the highest-risk configuration in this chapter, and it is
one setting away from the pipe-a-stranger-into-your-shell hazard
of Chapter 7. In that combination an agent can fetch a script and
run it with no pause, and it can be induced to do so by content it
reads rather than by anything you asked. If a task needs the
network, keep approvals on for the commands that use it, so the
fetch-and-run step surfaces for your review the way a `curl | sh`
would if you typed it yourself. Network on and approvals off, on a
machine with your real credentials, is the arrangement the vendors
reserve for disposable sandboxes, and so should you.
:::

## Secrets in prompts {.tier-beginner}

Chapter 10 taught that a secret leaks not only through Git but
through shell history, logs, and screenshots. An agent adds a new
and easy-to-forget channel: the prompt itself. Everything you
type to an agent, and much of what it reads to answer you, may be
sent to a model provider, written to a local log, and kept in a
session transcript. Paste an API key, a database password, or a
row of confidential data into the chat to "give it context," and
you have copied that secret into at least one place you did not
intend and possibly several.

The mechanics are concrete, not hypothetical. Gemini CLI has a
`telemetry.logPrompts` setting that, when on, writes the content
of your prompts to its logs, and it keeps a per-project shell-
command history under `~/.gemini/tmp/`, both current as of
2026-07-01. Other tools may retain session transcripts or history
of their own, so check each tool's behavior rather than assuming.
None of that is malicious; it is ordinary logging, and it is
exactly why a secret you paste does not simply vanish when the
session ends.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
Treat an agent's prompt as a public channel, the same way
Chapter 10 treats your shell history. Never paste a credential,
token, or confidential data value into a chat; instead point the
agent at the *mechanism*, "read the key from the `API_KEY`
environment variable," "the data is in `data/raw/`, which is
git-ignored," so it wires up the reference without ever seeing the
value. Keep secrets in environment variables or a secrets manager
(Chapter 10), keep confidential data out of the agent's scope (the
section above), and if a tool offers prompt logging or telemetry,
know its default and turn off what you do not want retained. A key
that reaches a transcript should be rotated, not hoped over.
:::

## Review discipline: diff before you accept {.tier-beginner}

The habit that makes agent assistance safe is the one the
companion Git book already argues for human collaborators: read
the change before you accept it. An agent's edit is a diff, and a
diff is exactly what you know how to review. The reproducible
pipeline gives this teeth, because a one-line edit can silently
change a result. Suppose you ask an agent to "tidy up the
portfolio script" and it decides, helpfully, to switch the
long-short sort from deciles to quintiles. Copied out of the real
`scripts/02_portfolio.py` and edited in a scratch directory, the
change it proposes is one line:

```bash
$ diff -U 0 -L a/02_portfolio.py -L b/02_portfolio.py \
    02_portfolio.py.orig 02_portfolio.py
--- a/02_portfolio.py
+++ b/02_portfolio.py
@@ -23 +23 @@
-N_PORT = 10  # deciles
+N_PORT = 5  # quintiles
```

That is a perfectly reasonable-looking edit and it would change
the study. The locked long-short alpha this book reproduces
comes from a decile sort; redefine the portfolios and every
number downstream moves. Read as a diff, the change is obvious in
two seconds. Accepted unread because the agent's explanation
sounded sensible, it becomes a result that no longer matches the
method you think you ran. The review is not bureaucratic; it is
the difference between those two outcomes.

::: {.callout-tip .recovery title="RECOVERY"}
If you accepted an agent's change and something downstream looks
wrong, the recovery is the review you skipped, run after the fact.
For anything the companion Git book had you commit, `git diff` and
`git restore` show and undo exactly what changed, which is the
strongest argument for committing a clean baseline *before* you
turn an agent loose on a file: a tracked file makes any edit,
yours or an agent's, reviewable and reversible. Run an agent in
auto-edit rather than full-trust mode so its edits accumulate as
reviewable diffs instead of already-run commands, and commit in
small steps so the diff you review is small. For an untracked file
an agent overwrote, the recovery story is the same bleak Chapter 7
described: there may not be one.
:::

## Reproducibility of agent-run commands {.tier-advanced}

An agent is fast, which tempts you to let it *do* things rather
than *record* things, and that temptation is the quiet enemy of
the workflow this book is about. A command an agent ran in a chat
session, and that you approved with a keystroke, has left no trace
in your project once the session scrolls away. If it was a step
that matters, generating a file, transforming data, producing a
result, then a month later you have an output and no committed
record of how it was made. That is precisely the "I think the
paper matches the data" state Chapter 12 built the Makefile to
escape.

The resolution is to make the agent serve the pipeline rather
than bypass it. When an agent works out the command that cleans a
file or builds a figure, the durable move is not to let it run
that command loose; it is to have it write the command *into* the
Makefile or a numbered script, where it becomes a reviewable,
version-controlled, re-runnable part of the graph. The agent is
then doing what it is genuinely good at, drafting the automation,
while the automation, not the chat transcript, remains the source
of truth.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
An agent-run command that is not written into your scripts or
Makefile is a step that vanishes when the session ends, and a
pipeline with vanished steps is not reproducible, however good the
agent was. The test is the one from Chapter 12: could someone
clone the repository and rebuild your result with `make`, with no
agent involved? Keep it true by treating an agent as an author of
automation, not a substitute for it, every command that matters
lands in a tracked file, and `make check` still re-derives the
locked data hash and alpha to catch any drift an agent's edit
introduced. The agent speeds up writing the pipeline; it does not
get to be the pipeline.
:::

## What an agent must never run unsupervised {.tier-beginner}

Most of an agent's work is low-stakes and reversible, and for
that work a looser posture is a reasonable trade. A short list of
actions is neither, and these should never run without a human
looking at the specific command first, no matter how much you
trust the agent's track record on ordinary edits:

- Anything that deletes broadly or irreversibly: `rm -rf` on a
  path outside a throwaway build directory, a `git reset --hard`
  or `git clean -fd` that discards uncommitted work, a mass delete
  on cloud storage.
- Anything that fetches and runs remote code: `curl | sh`, an
  install script, a `pip`/`npm` install from an unpinned or
  unfamiliar source (Chapter 7).
- Anything that touches credentials or access: writing to
  `~/.ssh`, editing shell startup files, changing file
  permissions broadly with `chmod -R`, printing a secret into the
  terminal or a file (Chapter 10).
- Anything that leaves your machine irreversibly: a `git push
  --force`, a deploy, a database migration, sending data to an
  external endpoint.

That these are dangerous for an *agent* is not a special rule;
they are the same operations this book flags for *you*, which is
the point. An agent has no more standing to run them blind than
you do.

::: {.callout-caution .danger title="DANGER"}
The trust-everything modes remove exactly the pause that would
catch the actions above, and the vendors are blunt about the risk,
though each frames it differently, current as of 2026-07-01.
Claude Code's `bypassPermissions` is documented for use only in
isolated containers or VMs. Codex's full bypass,
`--dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox` (alias `--yolo`), is
labeled in its own docs as dangerous and meant only for
environments that are already externally sandboxed. Gemini's
`yolo` auto-approves every tool call but turns its own sandbox on
by default, a different trade that is still no substitute for
review.
If you ever have a reason to run one, put it behind a sandbox that
cannot reach anything you care about, and never on the laptop that
holds your keys and your only copy of a dataset. The convenience
of never being asked is not worth one unreviewed command from the
list above.
:::

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

These use the asset-pricing project from Chapter 6 and the
pipeline from Chapter 12. Do them with an agent set to ask or
auto-edit mode, never full trust, and on a copy or a committed
tree so every change is reversible.

1. Find the approval setting. Open your agent's `--help` and its
   official docs and locate the flag that controls approvals and
   its current values. Write down, in one line, what your default
   posture is and what the trust-everything value is called in
   *your* version. This is the check the DIVERGENCE callout says
   to run every time.

2. Set the scope on purpose. `cd` into the asset-pricing project,
   confirm with `pwd`, and start the agent there. Ask it what it
   can write versus what it can read. Confirm its *write* scope is
   bounded to the project, and note whether its read scope reaches
   wider, so you know which boundary keeps data out of reach.

3. Review a real diff. Ask the agent to make one small change to
   `scripts/02_portfolio.py` (for example, add a comment). Before
   accepting, read the diff the way the review section did.
   Accept it, then run `git diff` to confirm the change on disk is
   exactly what you reviewed.

4. Make it reproducible. Ask the agent to add a `.PHONY` target to
   the Makefile that runs `make check`, rather than running the
   check for you in the chat. Confirm the new target is a tracked,
   reviewable line in the Makefile and that `make check` still
   re-derives the locked data hash and alpha.

5. Practice the refusal. Ask the agent to delete the `data/clean/`
   directory. Watch the approval prompt appear, read it, and
   decline. Articulate, in one sentence, why you would not want
   this exact prompt auto-approved, and connect it to Chapter 7's
   rule about `rm -rf`.
